sourcePatterns.description: Context menu disabled via addEventListener and preventDefault — common anti-analysis technique in phishing kits
sourcePatterns.note: Listed in rule writing guide as NOT in ruleset. Moderate weight because some legitimate image-protection sites do this, but very common in phishkits to prevent source inspection.

sourcePatterns.description: Right-click disabled via inline oncontextmenu returning false
sourcePatterns.note: Listed in rule writing guide as NOT in ruleset. Low weight — appears on some image-protection sites too, but stacks with other anti-analysis signals on phishing pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Keyboard shortcut interception blocking Ctrl+U (view source) — phishkit anti-forensics
sourcePatterns.note: Ctrl+U blocking is highly specific to phishing pages — legitimate sites have no reason to prevent viewing source code. Separate from DevTools/F12 blocking already in ruleset.

sourcePatterns.description: Keyboard shortcut interception blocking Ctrl+S (save page) — phishkit anti-forensics
sourcePatterns.note: Blocking page save combined with ctrlKey check is phishkit-specific anti-forensics. Some web apps override Ctrl+S for saving data, but the combination with preventDefault in the keyboard handler is a strong indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Keyboard shortcut interception blocking Ctrl+Shift+I/J/C (DevTools variations) — phishkit anti-analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Blocks DevTools opening via Ctrl+Shift+I, Ctrl+Shift+J, or Ctrl+Shift+C. Distinct from the existing devtools-block rule which covers F12/keyCode 123. These alternative shortcut blocks are characteristic of Whisper 2FA and similar advanced phishing kits.

sourcePatterns.description: Infinite debugger statement loop used to crash browser DevTools — anti-analysis watchdog technique
sourcePatterns.note: The setInterval + debugger combo creates a watchdog loop that freezes DevTools when opened. No legitimate website uses setInterval to repeatedly trigger debugger statements. Highly specific to phishing kits like Whisper 2FA.

sourcePatterns.description: Function constructor used to generate debugger traps dynamically — advanced anti-debugging technique
sourcePatterns.note: Using Function constructor to create debugger traps is a hallmark of obfuscated phishing kits. Legitimate code never dynamically constructs debugger statements via Function(). 25 literal characters provides strong specificity.

sourcePatterns.description: Checks navigator.webdriver property to detect headless browsers and automated scanners
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing kits check navigator.webdriver to detect headless Chrome and automated security scanners. Some legitimate bot protection does this, but combined with other signals it is a strong phishing indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Console object manipulation to detect DevTools open state — phishkit anti-analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Overriding console properties via defineProperty is used by phishkits like Whisper 2FA to detect when DevTools console is opened. If detected, the page wipes its content. Legitimate sites don't redefine console properties.

sourcePatterns.description: User selection disabled across entire page body or html element — prevents text copy for analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Disabling text selection on the entire page body prevents analysts from easily copying text for investigation. Low weight because some legitimate sites do this, but it stacks with other anti-analysis signals.

sourcePatterns.description: CSS disabling both text selection and copy across page — anti-forensics technique
sourcePatterns.note: Combining user-select:none with webkit-user-drag:none is overkill for legitimate purposes. This combination prevents any form of content copying or dragging, which is used by phishing kits to prevent analysis.

sourcePatterns.description: Timing-based bot detection measuring interaction speed — phishkit anti-automation
sourcePatterns.note: Measuring time between page load and user interaction to detect bots is a phishing kit anti-automation technique. Pages require minimum human-speed interaction before revealing the phishing content.

sourcePatterns.description: Mouse movement tracking required before revealing phishing content
sourcePatterns.note: Requiring mouse movement before revealing login forms is an anti-bot technique. Automated scanners that just load the page won't see the phishing content. The mousemove-to-reveal-form chain is specific to phishkits.

sourcePatterns.description: Page content wiped or replaced when DevTools detection fires — anti-forensics technique
sourcePatterns.note: Wiping page content (body.innerHTML = '', document.write('')) when DevTools or debugger is detected is a phishkit self-destruct mechanism. Legitimate sites never wipe themselves based on debugging state.

sourcePatterns.description: HTTP referer/referrer validation that blocks or redirects direct visits — scanner evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for an empty referrer and redirecting away is a scanner evasion technique. Security scanners navigate directly to the URL without a referrer, so the phishing page redirects them to a benign site.

sourcePatterns.description: User agent filtering to show phishing content only to mobile browsers
sourcePatterns.note: Filtering by mobile user agent is a phishing technique targeting mobile users specifically. SMS phishing (smishing) campaigns show phishing only to mobile browsers and redirect desktop/scanner visits to benign sites.

sourcePatterns.description: Geolocation API used to gate phishing content display by country
sourcePatterns.note: Using the Geolocation API to determine the victim's country and conditionally display phishing content is a targeted campaign technique. Pages show benign content to non-target countries and phishing to targets.

sourcePatterns.description: Screen resolution and color depth fingerprinting combined — bot detection via display properties
sourcePatterns.note: Collecting screen resolution and color depth together is a browser fingerprinting technique used by phishing kits to detect headless browsers (which often report unusual display properties). Low weight because analytics scripts do this too.

sourcePatterns.description: Empty referrer check redirecting to legitimate site — evasion of direct URL visits from security scanners
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting away when document.referrer is empty blocks direct URL visits (how security scanners access pages). The phishing page only works when accessed through the original phishing link.

sourcePatterns.description: Hidden form field named botcheck or honeypot — bot detection mechanism in phishing kit
sourcePatterns.note: Hidden fields specifically named 'botcheck' or 'honeypot' are phishing kit bot detection mechanisms. If a bot fills these invisible fields, the kit discards the submission. Legitimate sites use standard CAPTCHA services.

sourcePatterns.description: Script element removes itself from DOM after execution — DOM vanishing technique used by Tycoon 2FA
sourcePatterns.note: The DOM Vanishing Act technique has scripts remove themselves from the DOM after execution, leaving no trace for security tools that inspect the page's source post-render. This is a signature Tycoon 2FA technique.

sourcePatterns.description: Detection of Selenium, WebDriver, or Puppeteer automation frameworks
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing kits check for Selenium/WebDriver/Puppeteer/PhantomJS/Nightmare automation framework globals to detect security scanners. If detected, the page redirects to a benign site. Multiple framework checks in one page is highly specific to anti-analysis code.

sourcePatterns.description: Detection of Burp Suite penetration testing proxy — anti-analyst evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for Burp Suite strings is a definitive anti-analyst behavior. No legitimate website checks for the presence of security testing tools. This is used by Tycoon 2FA and Whisper 2FA to detect researcher environments.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirect to about:blank when security tool or sandbox is detected — Tycoon 2FA evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting to about:blank when a security tool is detected is a Tycoon 2FA signature behavior. When the kit detects debugger, Selenium, or Burp Suite, it sends the browser to a blank page to prevent analysis.

sourcePatterns.description: Timing-based debugger detection — measures execution delay to detect breakpoints
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon 2FA measures time before and after a debugger statement. If the debugger pauses execution for more than 0.1 seconds, it assumes a researcher is present and redirects to google.com. This timing+debugger+redirect chain is definitive anti-analysis.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirect to google.com or other major site when bot/sandbox check fails — phishing kit bailout
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting to Google, Bing, Rakuten, or Tesla when anti-bot checks fail is a common phishing kit bailout. Tycoon 2FA redirects to google.com on debugger detection; other kits use Rakuten or Tesla. The redirect to a benign popular site hides the phishing from analysts.

sourcePatterns.description: Copy event listener intercepting clipboard operations — prevents copying page content for analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Intercepting the copy event prevents analysts from copying page content for investigation. Phishing kits block copy operations to prevent URL, text, or source code from being easily shared with security teams.

sourcePatterns.description: Paste event listener intercepting paste operations — prevents password manager paste
sourcePatterns.note: Blocking paste operations prevents password managers from filling in credentials. Phishing kits do this because password managers often detect that the current domain doesn't match the saved credential's domain.

sourcePatterns.description: Text selection event blocked via selectstart listener — prevents content analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Blocking text selection via the selectstart event prevents analysts from selecting and copying text content. Combined with copy blocking and right-click disabling, this creates a full anti-analysis lock on page content.

sourcePatterns.description: Page content hidden or wiped when tab loses focus — anti-tab-switching analysis technique
sourcePatterns.note: Detecting tab visibility changes and hiding/wiping content when the user switches tabs prevents screenshot tools and analysis in other tabs from capturing the phishing page. This is advanced anti-forensics.

sourcePatterns.description: Print stylesheet hides page content — prevents printing phishing page for evidence
sourcePatterns.note: Print-specific CSS that hides all content prevents users and analysts from printing the phishing page as evidence. Legitimate sites don't hide their entire content when printing.

sourcePatterns.description: Drag and drop events disabled across page — prevents content extraction via dragging
sourcePatterns.note: Disabling drag/drop prevents analysts from dragging page content into other applications for analysis. This is a supporting anti-analysis signal that stacks with copy/paste/select blocking.

sourcePatterns.description: Page redirects or hides when window loses focus — anti-analysis tab-switching detection
sourcePatterns.note: Detecting window blur (losing focus) and redirecting or wiping content is an anti-analysis technique. When an analyst switches to another window to take notes or use tools, the phishing page destroys itself.

sourcePatterns.description: Console repeatedly cleared to hide debugging output — anti-analysis technique
sourcePatterns.note: Repeatedly clearing the console via setInterval prevents analysts from seeing debug output, network errors, or console warnings that might reveal the phishing kit's behavior.

sourcePatterns.description: MutationObserver watching for DOM changes by security tools — anti-tampering defense
sourcePatterns.note: Using MutationObserver to detect DOM modifications and self-destruct or redirect is an anti-tampering technique. If a security tool injects elements into the page, the observer detects it and triggers evasion.

sourcePatterns.description: Image error handler used to detect DevTools open state — covert debugging detection
sourcePatterns.note: Creating an Image object and overriding its id/toString property with Object.defineProperty to detect DevTools is a known Whisper 2FA technique. When DevTools tries to display the object, the getter fires and detects the open console.

sourcePatterns.description: Window size comparison used to detect DevTools panel — outerWidth vs innerWidth check
sourcePatterns.note: Comparing outerWidth/outerHeight to innerWidth/innerHeight detects whether DevTools is docked to the side or bottom of the browser window (the difference exceeds a threshold). This is a stealthier alternative to debugger-based detection.

sourcePatterns.description: Performance API entries checked for debugging or sandboxed environment markers
sourcePatterns.note: Analyzing performance entries to detect sandboxed or instrumented environments is an advanced anti-analysis technique. Automated tools often leave distinctive performance entry patterns.

sourcePatterns.description: WebRTC used to discover victim's real IP address — sandbox/VPN detection fingerprint
sourcePatterns.note: Using WebRTC to discover the victim's real IP address (bypassing VPN/proxy) is a fingerprinting technique used by phishing kits to detect security researchers who browse via VPN or corporate proxy.

sourcePatterns.description: Global error handler suppresses all JavaScript errors — hides phishing kit debug output
sourcePatterns.note: Setting window.onerror to always return true suppresses all JavaScript errors from appearing in the console. Phishing kits use this to prevent error messages from revealing the kit's internal operation.

sourcePatterns.description: Email domain validation filtering out personal email providers — enterprise-targeted phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Rejecting free email providers (Gmail, Yahoo, Hotmail, AOL) and only accepting corporate email addresses is a Sneaky 2FA technique that specifically targets enterprise users. This filtering is a definitive phishing signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Page CSS designed exclusively for small/mobile screens — smishing page targeting mobile
sourcePatterns.note: Pages styled exclusively for mobile screen widths (400-599px) with login/payment forms are SMS phishing pages. They're designed to look good only on phones because victims arrive via text message links.

sourcePatterns.description: Viewport meta tag with user-scalable=no and fixed width — mobile phishing page lockdown
sourcePatterns.note: Viewport locked to device-width with scaling disabled prevents users from zooming out to see the full page URL or inspect the page. Supporting signal for mobile-targeting phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: Email address validated against external API before showing phishing content — targeted phishing gate
sourcePatterns.note: Validating the victim's email via API before showing phishing content ensures only real corporate email users see the phishing page. Sneaky 2FA and similar enterprise-targeted kits use this to filter out security researchers.

sourcePatterns.description: Email domain checked against corporate domain list before proceeding — enterprise targeting filter
sourcePatterns.note: Checking the email domain and rejecting free providers (Gmail, Yahoo, Hotmail) is the Sneaky 2FA enterprise targeting filter. The phishing page only works for corporate email addresses.

sourcePatterns.description: WordPress login cookie checked to avoid showing malware to site admins
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for WordPress admin cookies and hiding malicious content from logged-in site admins is a SocGholish/ClearFake evasion technique. The site appears normal to the admin but shows fake updates to visitors.

sourcePatterns.description: Browser history flooded to prevent back button from leaving page — page lock technique
sourcePatterns.note: Flooding browser history with pushState/replaceState entries in a loop prevents the back button from working. Victims can't navigate away from tech support scam or phishing pages using the back button.

sourcePatterns.description: Beforeunload event preventing page exit with confirmation dialog — page retention technique
sourcePatterns.note: Setting a beforeunload handler with returnValue or preventDefault shows a 'Are you sure you want to leave?' dialog. Tech support scams and phishing pages use this to prevent victims from closing the tab.

sourcePatterns.description: Canvas fingerprinting — drawing hidden shapes to generate unique browser fingerprint
sourcePatterns.note: Canvas fingerprinting draws hidden text/shapes and converts to a data URL to create a unique browser fingerprint. Phishing kits use this to track victims and detect sandbox environments with non-standard renderers.

sourcePatterns.description: WebGL renderer and vendor queried for browser fingerprinting — sandbox detection
sourcePatterns.note: Querying WebGL renderer and vendor strings detects virtual machines and headless browsers that report generic GPU names like 'SwiftShader' or 'llvmpipe'. Phishing kits use this to filter out security scanners.

sourcePatterns.description: AudioContext fingerprinting — oscillator output analyzed for unique browser signature
sourcePatterns.note: AudioContext fingerprinting creates an oscillator and analyzes its output to generate a unique browser fingerprint. Each browser/OS combination produces slightly different audio processing results.

sourcePatterns.description: Timezone and locale checked to target specific geographic regions — targeted phishing gate
sourcePatterns.note: Checking timezone and browser language together to gate content by geography is a targeted phishing technique. Pages show phishing content only to victims in the target country and redirect others.

sourcePatterns.description: Network connection type checked to verify mobile device — smishing page verification
sourcePatterns.note: Checking navigator.connection for cellular network types verifies the visitor is on a mobile device. Smishing pages use this to ensure only mobile users (who arrived via SMS link) see the phishing content.

sourcePatterns.description: Touch event support checked to verify mobile device — smishing page filter
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for touch event support to verify the visitor is on a mobile/tablet device. SMS phishing pages that should only be viewed on phones use this to redirect desktop visitors.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirect to google.com/404 when bot or invalid access detected — Mamba 2FA antibot
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting to google.com/404 on invalid access or bot detection is a specific Mamba 2FA antibot signature. The kit redirects to Google's 404 page rather than google.com homepage.

sourcePatterns.description: localStorage and sessionStorage cleared on page unload — anti-forensics evidence destruction
sourcePatterns.note: Clearing all storage when the page unloads destroys forensic evidence of the phishing interaction. Credentials staged in storage during multi-step flows are wiped when the victim navigates away.

sourcePatterns.description: Service worker caches deleted to prevent cached content analysis
sourcePatterns.note: Deleting service worker caches prevents forensic analysis of cached phishing page content. After the attack, cached evidence is destroyed so investigators can't reconstruct the phishing page.

sourcePatterns.description: IntersectionObserver delays phishing form injection until element is visible — lazy-load evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Using IntersectionObserver to inject the phishing form only when the user scrolls to that part of the page delays form insertion until actual user interaction. Security scanners that don't scroll won't trigger the form injection.

sourcePatterns.description: IdleDetector API used to trigger phishing after user returns — attention-based triggering
sourcePatterns.note: Using the IdleDetector API to wait until the user returns from being idle before showing phishing content ensures the victim is actively looking at the screen. This maximizes the chance of credential entry.

sourcePatterns.description: Desktop user agents redirected to different content than mobile — mobile-only phishing page
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for desktop user agents and redirecting or hiding content ensures only mobile users see the phishing page. Security researchers primarily use desktop browsers, so this filters them out.

sourcePatterns.description: Screen width checked to verify mobile device — shows phishing only on small screens
sourcePatterns.note: Checking screen width to only show phishing content on mobile-sized screens (< 768px) is a smishing page technique. The page shows a blank or benign page on desktop to evade scanning.

sourcePatterns.description: Device orientation/motion events checked for mobile bot detection — accelerometer verification
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for device orientation/motion sensor data verifies the page is running on a real mobile device with an accelerometer. Emulators and desktop browsers don't produce real sensor data.

sourcePatterns.description: Pull-to-refresh gesture disabled via overscroll-behavior — prevents mobile page reload
sourcePatterns.note: Disabling pull-to-refresh via overscroll-behavior on a login page prevents mobile users from accidentally refreshing the page, which could reset the phishing state. Also prevents the user from seeing the URL flash during refresh.

sourcePatterns.description: Virtual keyboard presence detected via viewport resize — verifies real mobile interaction
sourcePatterns.note: Detecting the virtual keyboard opening by monitoring viewport height changes (the viewport shrinks when the keyboard appears) verifies the user is on a real mobile device. Emulators don't consistently replicate this behavior.

sourcePatterns.description: Performance/Resource Timing API data collected — proxy latency fingerprinting or analysis evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Collecting Performance/Resource Timing API data on a login page can detect proxy latency or be used to profile the victim's connection. AiTM kits may collect timing data to verify their proxy is working correctly or to exfiltrate network fingerprints.

sourcePatterns.description: CSS user-select:none applied broadly — prevents text selection for anti-copy/anti-forensics
sourcePatterns.note: Applying user-select:none to the entire page body prevents users from selecting and copying text. Phishing pages do this to prevent forensic text extraction and to make it harder to search for the page's text content.

sourcePatterns.description: navigator.webdriver check for Selenium/automation detection — Tycoon anti-bot evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Checking navigator.webdriver for true (set by Selenium/Puppeteer), or checking for Selenium/PhantomJS global objects, detects automated browsers. Tycoon redirects to about:blank or a decoy site when automation is detected.

sourcePatterns.description: PhantomJS headless browser detection — Tycoon checks for PhantomJS-specific globals
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon checks for PhantomJS-specific window properties (_phantom, callPhantom) to detect headless browser analysis. PhantomJS is used by some security scanning tools.

sourcePatterns.description: Clipboard data overwritten on copy event — Tycoon prevents page content extraction
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon intercepts the copy event and overwrites the clipboard data with garbage or empty string, preventing security researchers from copying page source text for analysis. This is distinct from the right-click disable pattern.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirect to Rakuten homepage when analysis tool detected — Tycoon-specific decoy redirect
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon 2FA specifically redirects to Rakuten's homepage (rakuten.com or rakuten.co.jp) when analysis tools or debugging are detected. This exact redirect target is a definitive Tycoon 2FA signature — no other kit uses Rakuten as a decoy.

sourcePatterns.description: F12 key blocked to prevent DevTools opening — Tycoon's developer tools prevention
sourcePatterns.note: Blocking F12 (keyCode 123) to prevent DevTools opening is a specific Tycoon anti-analysis technique added in December 2024. Combined with Ctrl+Shift+I and Ctrl+U blocking (which we already detect), this covers all DevTools entry points.

sourcePatterns.note: Combination of Amazon-specific CSS classes and JavaScript files that only appear on Amazon impersonation sites

sourcePatterns.description: Adobe PDF document verification page requesting login credentials to access protected files
sourcePatterns.note: Combines Adobe PDF branding with file verification pretext and password collection - legitimate Adobe services don't require arbitrary logins for PDF access

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft sign-in page UI elements and terminology impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: Combines Microsoft's distinctive 'Sign in to your account' header with secondary UI elements or domain references

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft MFA authentication flow text — 'Approve sign in request' and organization sign-in page language used in Microsoft login impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: These exact phrases are from Microsoft's MFA/authenticator flow. Legitimate occurrences are only on login.microsoftonline.com and login.microsoft.com. Finding both strings together on any other domain is a strong Microsoft impersonation signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft-specific 'Other ways to sign in' text combined with password field — Microsoft login page impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: The phrase 'Other ways to sign in' is specific to Microsoft's login UX. Combined with a password field on a non-Microsoft domain, this is a strong impersonation signal. Google uses 'More ways to sign in' and other providers use different wording.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft-specific 'Stay signed in?' post-authentication prompt found alongside sign-in elements
sourcePatterns.note: The 'Stay signed in?' prompt with checkbox is specific to Microsoft's KMSI (Keep Me Signed In) flow. Finding this alongside other login elements on a non-Microsoft domain indicates a full authentication flow clone. Weight kept moderate as some SSO integrations may embed this legitimately.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft-specific sign-in flow text phrases appearing together, indicating brand impersonation
sourcePatterns.patternString: Sign in with a security key[\s\S]{0,3000}Sign-in options|Sign-in options[\s\S]{0,3000}Sign in with a security key
sourcePatterns.note: The phrase 'Sign in with a security key' combined with 'Sign-in options' is specific to Microsoft's login flow. Legitimate Microsoft domains are excluded by the domain allowlist. Non-Microsoft domains showing this exact combination are impersonating the Microsoft login experience.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft login page impersonation with 'No account? Create one!' and sign-in elements
sourcePatterns.patternString: No account\?\s*Create one![\s\S]{0,5000}(?:Sign-in options|sign in with a security key)
sourcePatterns.note: The exact phrasing 'No account? Create one!' is Microsoft-specific copy used on their login page. Combined with other Microsoft sign-in elements on a non-Microsoft domain, this is strong evidence of brand impersonation. This text combination does not appear on other major login providers.

sourcePatterns.description: Clearbit Logo API used to dynamically fetch victim's company logo for targeted phishing
sourcePatterns.note: The Clearbit Logo API lets phishing kits dynamically load any company's logo based on the victim's email domain, enabling mass personalization. Legitimate login pages use their own static assets, not third-party logo APIs.

sourcePatterns.description: Azure AD CDN used to dynamically load branding based on victim email domain — personalized phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon 2FA and other Microsoft-targeting kits dynamically load logos and backgrounds from Microsoft's AADCDN based on the victim's email domain. This makes the phishing page match the victim's organization's real login branding. The AADCDN + branding/logo combination on a non-Microsoft domain is highly suspicious.

sourcePatterns.description: CSS custom properties set to exact brand colors of major companies — pixel-perfect impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: CSS custom properties using exact hex values for Microsoft (#0078d4), Facebook (#1877f2), Google (#ea4335), Amazon (#ff9900), etc. indicate brand color impersonation. Low weight because legitimate brand-themed sites exist, but stacks as a supporting signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Major brand logo embedded as inline SVG to avoid external image loading detection
sourcePatterns.note: Embedding brand logos as inline SVG avoids making external requests that could be tracked or blocked. Phishing kits inline SVG brand logos so the page looks legitimate without loading any external assets.

sourcePatterns.description: Theme-color meta tag set to match brand colors — mobile browser chrome impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: Setting the theme-color meta tag to exact brand colors (Microsoft blue, Facebook blue, Google red, Amazon orange, Spotify green, Netflix red) makes the mobile browser's address bar and status bar match the impersonated brand.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft corporate branding assets loaded from non-Microsoft CDN — proxied branding leak
sourcePatterns.note: Microsoft's corporate branding CDN assets (aadcdn.msftauth.net, logincdn.msftauth.net) loading on a page with a login form means the proxy is serving Microsoft's real branding. Legitimate Microsoft login only serves these assets from Microsoft domains.

sourcePatterns.description: Copyright text contains a well-known brand name — brand impersonation in page footer
sourcePatterns.note: Copyright text claiming ownership by a major brand (Microsoft, Google, Apple, etc.) on a page hosted on a different domain is a simple but effective brand impersonation indicator. Phishers copy the copyright footer from the real site.

sourcePatterns.description: Copyright year significantly outdated — phishing page copied from an old version of the target site
sourcePatterns.note: A copyright year from 2010-2023 (significantly older than the current year 2026) suggests the phishing page was cloned from an older snapshot of the target site. Phishers don't update the copyright year when they copy login pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Brand logo embedded as base64 data URI — self-contained phishing page with no external asset dependencies
sourcePatterns.note: A large base64-encoded image (logo) on a page with a password field means the phishing page is completely self-contained — no external asset loads that could trigger network-level detection. The logo is embedded to avoid referencing the real brand's CDN.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake package tracking status UI with progress bar/steps — courier tracking page impersonation
sourcePatterns.note: Fake delivery tracking status UIs showing package progress (in transit, processing, out for delivery) with a progress bar/steps UI create believable courier page impersonation. The tracking UI is real-looking but the action (pay/verify) is the scam.

sourcePatterns.description: Courier brand logo/name in page header with payment or info form below — basic delivery phishing layout
sourcePatterns.note: A courier brand name or logo in the page header followed by a form with input fields is the basic layout of every delivery phishing page. This structural pattern catches the simplest delivery phish that have no obfuscation or JavaScript.

sourcePatterns.description: US toll system brand name on page with payment card form — toll service impersonation with card harvest
sourcePatterns.note: Any US toll system brand name (EZPass, SunPass, FasTrak, I-Pass, TxTag, PeachPass, ExpressToll, NTTA, EZDrive, Good To Go, RiverLink, PikePass) on a page with credit card input fields is toll service impersonation.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake reCAPTCHA-style verification hiding login form
Fake captcha checkbox verification commonly used in phishing pages
sourcePatterns.patternString: I am not a robot[^\r\n]{0,200}checkbox|checkbox[^\r\n]{0,200}I am not a robot

sourcePatterns.description: Detects fake Cloudflare-style 'Just a moment...' interstitial with spinner animation but no real Cloudflare challenge JS
Detects fake browser-check verification screen with checkbox that gates a hidden credential form, common in phishkits
French-language fake CAPTCHA verification page with form action to PHP — phishkit gating technique
Cloudflare Turnstile CAPTCHA loaded on a page — potential CAPTCHA gating for scanner evasion
sourcePatterns.note: Turnstile is used legitimately by many sites, hence low weight. But its presence on phishing pages serves as a scanner blocker — bots can't solve it. Stacks with other signals.

sourcePatterns.description: hCaptcha widget loaded — potential CAPTCHA gating for scanner evasion
sourcePatterns.note: hCaptcha is legitimately used but also heavily used by phishing kits as an anti-bot gate. Very low weight because many real sites use it, but it stacks with other indicators.

sourcePatterns.description: Google reCAPTCHA v2 invisible badge loaded — potential CAPTCHA gating on credential page
sourcePatterns.note: reCAPTCHA with render parameter indicates invisible reCAPTCHA which auto-verifies. Used by phishing kits as a lightweight bot filter. Very low weight because this is also extremely common on legitimate sites.

sourcePatterns.description: Custom CAPTCHA rendered via HTML5 canvas element — custom anti-bot gate replacing third-party CAPTCHAs
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon 2FA and similar kits have shifted from Cloudflare Turnstile to custom canvas-rendered CAPTCHAs to avoid fingerprinting. Drawing randomized text on canvas with noise/distortion for CAPTCHA verification is a phishing kit signature.

sourcePatterns.description: Google reCAPTCHA loaded with a site key on a non-matching domain — CAPTCHA proxy abuse
sourcePatterns.note: reCAPTCHA loaded with both render parameter and data-sitekey suggests the page is relaying CAPTCHA challenges. AiTM kits proxy the real site's reCAPTCHA to the victim and relay the solution back.

sourcePatterns.description: Touch-based fake CAPTCHA requiring touch/swipe interaction — mobile-specific bot filter
sourcePatterns.note: Touch-based fake CAPTCHAs require swipe or touch interactions that only work on real mobile devices. This filters out desktop scanners while creating a convincing verification experience for mobile victims.

sourcePatterns.description: Cloudflare Turnstile cf-turnstile widget div on a page with password/credential input fields
sourcePatterns.note: A cf-turnstile widget div with a real sitekey (0x prefix + 22+ alphanumeric chars) on the same page as a password input field is the #1 Turnstile phishing pattern. The Turnstile gates a credential harvesting form, blocking scanners but allowing human victims through.

sourcePatterns.description: Turnstile API script loaded on a page with password input — CAPTCHA-gated credential harvest
sourcePatterns.note: Loading the Turnstile v0 API script (challenges.cloudflare.com/turnstile/v0/api.js) on a page with a password field is the telltale phishkit integration. Legitimate login pages hosted behind Cloudflare use Managed Challenge, not embedded Turnstile widgets on credential forms.

sourcePatterns.description: turnstile.render() called explicitly to create widget before credential form interaction
sourcePatterns.note: Explicit turnstile.render() with a callback that reveals/enables a credential form is the programmatic Turnstile integration used by phishing kits. The callback fires after verification, enabling the hidden phishing form.

sourcePatterns.description: Turnstile success callback that shows or enables a hidden credential form — gate-then-reveal pattern
sourcePatterns.note: A Turnstile callback function that reveals a hidden form (display:block, removes 'hidden' class, enables disabled elements) is the gate-then-reveal pattern. The credential form only becomes visible/usable after Turnstile verification blocks scanners.

sourcePatterns.description: Turnstile explicit render with sitekey in JavaScript variable — programmatic widget creation
sourcePatterns.note: Explicit turnstile.render() with a sitekey starting with 0x is the programmatic Turnstile integration. Phishing kits use explicit rendering for more control over when the widget appears and what happens after verification.

sourcePatterns.description: Turnstile implicit rendering (auto-detect cf-turnstile class) on credential page — automated widget setup
sourcePatterns.note: The Turnstile script loaded with async/defer + a cf-turnstile class div + password input on the same page is implicit rendering on a credential form. The script auto-detects the cf-turnstile div and renders the widget.

sourcePatterns.description: Turnstile expired-callback handling that re-renders or refreshes widget on phishing page
sourcePatterns.note: Handling the Turnstile expired-callback to reset/re-render the widget shows the phishing kit is managing token lifecycle. Legitimate sites handle this server-side; client-side expired callback handling indicates a client-side-only phishing implementation.

sourcePatterns.description: Form submit blocked until Turnstile token is present — CAPTCHA validation gate on credential form
sourcePatterns.note: Checking for a valid Turnstile token before allowing form submission (preventing submit if token is missing) is the client-side enforcement pattern. The phishing kit ensures the victim completes Turnstile before credentials are submitted.

sourcePatterns.description: Cloudflare challenge-platform script loaded within Turnstile context — embedded challenge iframe
sourcePatterns.note: The cdn-cgi/challenge-platform scripts loaded alongside a cf-turnstile widget indicate the full Cloudflare challenge infrastructure is being invoked. On a phishing page, this creates a convincing Cloudflare-protected appearance.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form initially hidden with Turnstile verification required before display — full gate pattern
sourcePatterns.note: A Turnstile widget followed by a hidden form containing a password field is the complete phishing gate pattern. The form is display:none until the Turnstile callback fires, then it becomes visible. This is the most distinctive Turnstile phishing signature.

sourcePatterns.description: Tycoon 2FA custom canvas CAPTCHA — randomized characters with background noise and distortion
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon's custom CAPTCHA renders randomized characters on a canvas element with background noise and slight distortions, then validates user input. This replaced Cloudflare Turnstile starting April 2025. The canvas+randomization+verify chain is distinctive.

sourcePatterns.description: CAPTCHA success triggers fetch for phishing page payload — Tycoon's gate-then-load architecture
sourcePatterns.note: After successful CAPTCHA verification, Tycoon fetches the actual phishing page payload from an attacker-controlled server. The CAPTCHA gate→success→fetch-payload chain ensures security scanners that can't solve the CAPTCHA never see the credential form.

sourcePatterns.description: IconCaptcha integration — icon-based CAPTCHA used by Tycoon as a rotating verification method
sourcePatterns.note: IconCaptcha is an icon-based CAPTCHA where users select the correct icon from a set. Tycoon 2FA rotates between custom canvas CAPTCHA, Google reCAPTCHA, and IconCaptcha to avoid signature-based detection of any single CAPTCHA type.

sourcePatterns.description: Detects phishkit gate pattern: sets a cookie with timestamp then reloads page to pass server-side check
Fake Cloudflare 'checking your browser' challenge page created in JavaScript — impersonation of CDN security
sourcePatterns.note: Fake Cloudflare-style browser checks with timed reveals are used by phishing kits to gate content from scanners. The 'checking your browser' + setTimeout + reveal pattern is characteristic of fake CDN interstitials.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake Cloudflare Ray ID displayed to impersonate CDN protection page
sourcePatterns.note: Displaying a fake Cloudflare Ray ID alongside a 'security check' form is a phishing technique that impersonates CDN protection while actually gating a credential harvesting form.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake DDoS protection page with spinning/loading animation before revealing form
sourcePatterns.note: Fake DDoS protection pages with CSS animations that resolve to a login form are phishing gating techniques. Real DDoS protection never leads to a login form.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake CDN security check page impersonating Cloudflare, Sucuri, or similar WAF
sourcePatterns.note: Fake security check pages impersonating CDN/WAF providers are used to gate phishing content. The page shows a branded 'verification' page that resolves to a redirect to the actual phishing form.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake JavaScript challenge with computed proof-of-work before revealing content
sourcePatterns.note: Fake proof-of-work JavaScript challenges that appear to compute something before revealing content are phishing gates. They impersonate the behavior of real CDN JS challenges but the 'computation' is just a timer.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake cookie consent banner that gates phishing content — social engineering to bypass scanner
sourcePatterns.note: Fake cookie consent banners that must be clicked before the phishing form appears act as bot filters. Automated scanners that don't click the consent button never see the credential harvesting page.

sourcePatterns.description: Email Address/User ID label pattern common in email phishing
Form submission to formspree.io with password field
Detects Netflix logo usage with email collection forms
Fake PayPal OpenID Connect login endpoint
login_up.php filename - phishkit credential harvesting pattern
HSBC login form submitting to suspicious PHP endpoint
URL contains government agency keyword in hostname with payment-related path on non-.gov domain
Page references Office 365 and contains a password input field, strong indicator of O365 credential phishing
Detects phishkit pattern that intercepts form submit with preventDefault, then exfiltrates via fetch() before redirecting to form action URL
Detects URL patterns with campaign tracking key and username/email parameter, typical of gated phishing kits
Detects input fields with password-related placeholder or label but type='text' instead of type='password', a common phishkit trait
Detects phishing pages that track keystrokes, clicks, and time-on-page via inline counters — characteristic of phishing simulations and credential harvesting kits
Detects JS pattern that copies visible input values into hidden form fields for exfiltration, commonly used in phishkits to decouple display from submission
Form action pointing to facebook.com from non-Facebook domain, common phishkit decoy technique
Form action posts credentials to formsubmit.co, a free form-to-email relay commonly used for phishing exfiltration
SecurityIQ phishing simulation kit exfiltration endpoint /phished/dataEntered/ with UUID
SecurityIQ-style credential exfiltration callback path /phished/dataEntered/ with UUID, indicating phishing simulation or kit derivative
Outlook phishkit with pre-populated incorrect password error to encourage re-entry of credentials
Form with Spanish national document type options (NIF, CIF, NIE, Pasaporte) used in banking credential phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Spanish banking phishkits include a document type dropdown with NIF/CIF/NIE/Pasaporte options. This exact combination of all four Spanish ID types in a form selector is unique to Spanish bank login impersonation. Legitimate Spanish banks serve from .es domains or their own branded domains, not arbitrary TLDs. Multiple 3-9 char anchors (NIF, CIF, NIE, Pasaporte).

sourcePatterns.description: DRM parameter with hex token in credential harvesting URL structure
sourcePatterns.note: 64-character hex DRM tokens are characteristic of phishing campaign tracking - not used by legitimate services

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript handlers sync visible input values to hidden form fields across multiple forms
sourcePatterns.note: Phishkit pattern: visible inputs trigger handlers that sync values to hidden fields across all forms - legitimate sites don't need this cross-form synchronization

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript tracks boolean flag of password field entry rather than password value
sourcePatterns.note: Phishkit behavior: converting password presence to boolean flag for hidden field tracking - legitimate forms validate actual password values, not just presence

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript handlers copy visible form input values to hidden fields with matching credential field names
Form containing multiple password-type input fields suggesting credential harvesting across login steps
sourcePatterns.note: Phishkits that simulate multi-step login flows often render all password fields in a single page (hidden/shown via JS). Legitimate login pages rarely have two password inputs unless it is a password-change form. Combined with other signals this is a meaningful indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: FormData constructed and sent via fetch to a PHP endpoint — classic phishkit credential exfiltration pattern
sourcePatterns.note: Constructing FormData and sending it to a .php endpoint via fetch is a near-definitive phishkit credential exfiltration pattern. Modern legitimate login pages use JSON APIs, not PHP form handlers from JavaScript.

sourcePatterns.description: XMLHttpRequest opened to a PHP endpoint for credential exfiltration — common in older phishing kits
sourcePatterns.note: XMLHttpRequest to a PHP endpoint is the older equivalent of the fetch+PHP pattern. Very common in phishing kits from PhishTank/OpenPhish corpus. Legitimate sites use modern APIs, not raw XHR to PHP files.

sourcePatterns.description: jQuery AJAX POST call sending credential field values — common in jQuery-based phishing kits
sourcePatterns.note: jQuery AJAX/POST calls that include password field names destined for PHP endpoints are a strong phishkit signal. The jQuery+PHP combo is characteristic of older but still widely-deployed phishing kits.

sourcePatterns.description: Credentials serialized to JSON and sent via fetch POST — modern phishkit exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Serializing credentials to JSON then fetching is common in modern phishkits. Weight is moderate because legitimate login forms also POST JSON, but the stringify-with-password-then-fetch chain is distinctive.

sourcePatterns.description: Credentials base64-encoded with btoa before exfiltration — anti-detection technique
sourcePatterns.note: Using btoa() to base64-encode values extracted from password fields before sending is a phishkit anti-detection technique. Legitimate login pages never base64-encode credentials client-side before submission.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirect to legitimate brand site after credential capture — post-exfiltration concealment
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting to the real brand's legitimate site after a successful data exfiltration (in a .then(), success callback, or onload) is the classic phishkit concealment pattern. The victim thinks they were logged out and tries again on the real site.

sourcePatterns.description: HTML meta refresh redirecting to legitimate brand site — used after credential capture page
sourcePatterns.note: A meta refresh redirecting to a major brand's legitimate login page after a short delay is a strong indicator of a phishing landing page that has already captured credentials and is now sending the victim to the real site.

sourcePatterns.description: Hidden password input field using visibility or opacity tricks — honeypot or staged credential capture
sourcePatterns.note: A password field hidden via CSS positioning, visibility, or opacity indicates either a honeypot field for bot detection or staged credential capture. Phishing kits use both techniques.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form with password field dynamically injected via innerHTML — client-side cloaking technique
sourcePatterns.note: Dynamically injecting a password input field via innerHTML is a client-side cloaking technique used by phishing kits to evade detection systems that analyze static HTML. The form only appears after JavaScript execution.

sourcePatterns.description: Email address extracted from URL parameters and pre-filled into form — targeted phishing personalization
sourcePatterns.note: Extracting an email from URL parameters and pre-filling a form field is a personalization technique used by phishing campaigns. The victim sees their email already filled in, increasing credibility.

sourcePatterns.description: Multi-step authentication flow with staged email-then-password capture in client-side JavaScript
sourcePatterns.note: Client-side multi-step auth flows that show/hide stages using display/class/visibility manipulation combined with password field references indicate a phishing page mimicking Microsoft/Google stepped login.

sourcePatterns.description: OTP/MFA code capture field combined with credential field references — MFA bypass phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Page JavaScript referencing both OTP/MFA input fields and password fields indicates a credential harvesting page with MFA bypass capability, characteristic of Tycoon 2FA, Sneaky 2FA, and similar kits.

sourcePatterns.description: Form submission or fetch to a different domain than the page origin — credential exfiltration to third party
sourcePatterns.note: A form with an absolute URL action pointing to an external domain, containing a password field, indicates cross-origin credential submission. Legitimate login pages submit to their own domain. Moderate weight because some SSO flows use cross-origin POST.

sourcePatterns.description: Credentials stored in localStorage between multi-step phishing pages
sourcePatterns.note: Storing credential values in localStorage is used by multi-step phishing kits to persist data between page transitions (email step → password step → MFA step). Legitimate login pages use server-side sessions, not localStorage for credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Navigator.sendBeacon used for credential exfiltration — fire-and-forget data submission
sourcePatterns.note: sendBeacon() sends data asynchronously and is fire-and-forget — the browser sends it even if the page is being unloaded. Phishkits use it to guarantee exfiltration even if the user navigates away immediately. Legitimate login pages never use sendBeacon for credential submission.

sourcePatterns.description: Multiple short maxlength input fields for OTP/PIN capture — MFA harvesting UI
sourcePatterns.note: Four or more consecutive maxlength=1 input fields create an OTP/PIN entry UI. This is the classic 2FA code capture layout used by Tycoon 2FA, Sneaky 2FA, and similar MFA-bypassing phishing kits.

sourcePatterns.description: Password input field with randomized or obfuscated name attribute — evades field-name-based detection
sourcePatterns.note: Password fields with randomized short names like 'a839', 'x2847' indicate a phishing kit that obfuscates field names to evade detection rules that look for standard names like 'password'. Legitimate login pages use human-readable field names.

sourcePatterns.description: Autocomplete disabled on password field combined with non-standard name — anti-browser-autofill phishing technique
sourcePatterns.note: Disabling autocomplete on a password field that uses a non-standard name is a phishing technique to prevent browser autofill from revealing that this isn't the real site. Very low weight as supporting signal only.

sourcePatterns.description: Social Security Number input field pattern — sensitive PII harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: Input fields labeled for SSN with appropriate maxlength constraints indicate PII harvesting. Legitimate sites that collect SSN do so through established forms, not ad-hoc credential pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Credit card number, expiry, and CVV input fields on a non-commerce page — financial data harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: The trio of card number + expiry + CVV fields appearing together indicates a financial data harvesting form. Moderate weight because legitimate e-commerce sites have similar forms, but this stacks with other signals.

sourcePatterns.description: postMessage used for cross-origin credential exfiltration between phishing frames
sourcePatterns.note: Using postMessage to send credential data between frames or windows is an AiTM phishing exfiltration technique. The phishing page communicates stolen credentials to a parent/child frame on the attacker's domain.

sourcePatterns.description: WebSocket connection used for real-time credential streaming to attacker server
sourcePatterns.note: WebSocket connections for real-time credential streaming are used by AiTM phishing kits that need to relay credentials and MFA tokens to the attacker's server instantly. Legitimate login pages don't send passwords over WebSockets.

sourcePatterns.description: Fetch API call to absolute URL with credentials in body — cross-origin exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: A fetch() call to an absolute external URL that includes password field values in the request body is cross-origin credential exfiltration. Legitimate login pages fetch to relative URLs on their own domain.

sourcePatterns.description: IndexedDB used to persist credentials between phishing page stages
sourcePatterns.note: Using IndexedDB to persist credential data between phishing page stages is a more sophisticated alternative to localStorage. Multi-step phishing flows store email from step 1 to use in step 2.

sourcePatterns.description: Credentials stored in sessionStorage for multi-step phishing flow
sourcePatterns.note: SessionStorage is used by multi-step phishing kits to persist credential data within a browser session. Unlike localStorage, sessionStorage clears when the tab closes, leaving less forensic evidence.

sourcePatterns.description: Submit button disabled after first click with credential extraction — single-capture phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Disabling the submit button immediately after credential capture prevents the victim from resubmitting or testing different credentials. The phishing kit captures once then redirects.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript redirect triggered after form submission handler — post-harvest navigation
sourcePatterns.note: A form submit handler that triggers a delayed redirect via setTimeout is the standard phishkit flow: capture credentials, show a brief loading state, then redirect to the real brand site.

sourcePatterns.description: Seed phrase or recovery phrase input fields — cryptocurrency wallet theft
sourcePatterns.note: Input fields labeled for seed/recovery/mnemonic phrases are cryptocurrency wallet theft forms. No legitimate service asks users to enter their full seed phrase into a web form.

sourcePatterns.description: Private key input field — cryptocurrency or authentication key theft
sourcePatterns.note: Input fields for private keys are definitive crypto theft indicators. No legitimate wallet service asks users to paste their private key into a web form.

sourcePatterns.description: Input event listener on password field capturing keystrokes in real-time
sourcePatterns.note: Attaching an input event listener to password fields that immediately sends each keystroke to an external endpoint is real-time credential streaming. This is more aggressive than form submission interception.

sourcePatterns.description: Custom password visibility toggle with eye icon — polished phishing kit UI element
sourcePatterns.note: Custom password visibility toggle implementations (show/hide password eye icon) in inline JavaScript indicate a phishing kit recreating the login UI rather than using the native browser feature. Very low weight as supporting signal.

sourcePatterns.description: reCAPTCHA response token captured and exfiltrated alongside credentials
sourcePatterns.note: Capturing the reCAPTCHA response token and sending it to an external server alongside credentials is an AiTM technique. The attacker uses the victim's solved CAPTCHA token to replay it on the real site.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form using GET method exposing credentials in URL — insecure or phishing form
sourcePatterns.note: A login form using GET method exposes credentials in the URL query string. No legitimate login page uses GET for password submission. Phishing kits sometimes use GET to simplify their credential collection.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake WebAuthn/FIDO2 prompt impersonating browser security key dialog
sourcePatterns.note: Creating a fake WebAuthn/FIDO2 dialog to trick users into thinking they're using their security key when they're actually on a phishing page. The fake prompt captures the user's interaction while the real authentication happens on the attacker's server.

sourcePatterns.description: Custom data attribute storing victim email for tracking — personalized phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Custom HTML data attributes containing email addresses indicate personalized phishing where the victim's email is embedded in the page for tracking or form pre-fill. The page was generated specifically for this victim.

sourcePatterns.description: Session cookie captured and sent to external endpoint — AiTM session hijacking
sourcePatterns.note: Extracting document.cookie and sending it to an external endpoint with session/auth-related cookie names is AiTM session hijacking. ESTSAUTH is the Microsoft session cookie specifically targeted by Tycoon 2FA.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake OAuth consent screen requesting broad permissions — OAuth phishing technique
sourcePatterns.note: Fake OAuth consent screens requesting broad permissions (email access, contacts, calendar, files) are used to trick users into granting malicious apps access to their data without stealing passwords.

sourcePatterns.description: CSS attribute selectors with background-image on input values — CSS keylogging technique
sourcePatterns.note: CSS attribute selectors targeting input values with background-image URLs create a CSS keylogger. As the user types, each new character triggers a different CSS rule that loads a unique URL, logging each keystroke server-side without any JavaScript.

sourcePatterns.description: CDN domain used to front exfiltration requests — credential theft hidden behind trusted CDN
sourcePatterns.note: Sending credentials to CDN domains (CloudFront, Azure CDN, Akamai, Fastly) uses domain fronting to hide the exfiltration destination. The CDN domain is trusted, but the request routes to the attacker's origin server.

sourcePatterns.description: Page auto-redirects to legitimate site after timeout — self-destructing phishing page
sourcePatterns.note: A timed auto-redirect to a legitimate brand site after a delay (encoded as milliseconds like 300000 or 5*60*1000) is a self-destruct mechanism. The phishing page exists only temporarily, then becomes an innocent redirect.

sourcePatterns.description: Cross-origin iframe loading a login page from a different domain — credential capture via framing
sourcePatterns.note: A full-width, borderless iframe loading content from an external domain on a page with login elements is the GhostFrame technique. The outer page looks clean while the iframe contains the actual phishing form.

sourcePatterns.description: Multi-page form wizard collecting credentials across multiple steps — staged data harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: Multi-step form wizards that progressively collect credentials, then personal info (SSN, DOB), then payment details across separate 'steps' are comprehensive data harvesting phishing pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Socket.IO used to relay credentials in real-time — AiTM phishing WebSocket communication
sourcePatterns.note: Using Socket.IO to emit/send credential data is the signature real-time relay mechanism of Mamba 2FA and similar AiTM kits. Credentials are relayed instantly to the attacker's server for live session hijacking.

sourcePatterns.description: Shadow DOM used to hide credential harvesting form from page source inspection
sourcePatterns.note: Using closed Shadow DOM to inject login forms makes the form completely invisible to document.querySelector and outerHTML inspection. Security tools that analyze the main DOM tree cannot see the phishing form.

sourcePatterns.description: Window.name used to persist credential data across page navigations
sourcePatterns.note: Window.name persists across page navigations within the same tab. Phishing kits use it as a covert data channel to transfer stolen credentials between phishing domains without URL parameters or cookies.

sourcePatterns.description: Web Worker used for background credential exfiltration — invisible to page inspection
sourcePatterns.note: Creating an inline Web Worker from a Blob to exfiltrate credentials in a background thread. The Worker runs independently of the page and its network requests are harder to inspect. The credential data is sent to the Worker via postMessage.

sourcePatterns.description: Fetch with keepalive flag for fire-and-forget credential exfiltration on page unload
sourcePatterns.note: Using fetch() with keepalive:true ensures the request completes even if the page is being unloaded. This fire-and-forget technique guarantees credential exfiltration even if the user navigates away immediately after submitting.

sourcePatterns.description: Payment Request API invoked on a non-payment page — fake payment harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: Invoking the Payment Request API to display the browser's native payment sheet is a sophisticated phishing technique. The native payment UI is trusted by users, but on a phishing page it captures real payment details.

sourcePatterns.description: Magecart-style payment form skimmer listening on credit card input fields
sourcePatterns.note: Magecart-style skimmers attach event listeners to payment card input fields and exfiltrate the values on change/blur/keyup events. This pattern matches the listener-on-card-field-then-exfil chain.

sourcePatterns.description: Checkout form submission intercepted for payment data skimming — e-commerce attack
sourcePatterns.note: Intercepting checkout/payment form submission to skim card details is the classic Magecart attack pattern. The skimmer captures all payment fields on submit and sends them to an attacker-controlled endpoint.

sourcePatterns.description: Contenteditable div used as credential input instead of standard form — evasion technique
sourcePatterns.note: Using contenteditable divs instead of standard input elements for credential entry evades detection tools that look for <input type=password>. The div functions as an input field but doesn't trigger browser autofill or password manager features.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake passkey/WebAuthn prompt with password fallback — phishing attack on passwordless auth
sourcePatterns.note: Fake passkey prompts that 'fail' and fall back to password entry are designed to phish users who normally use passwordless authentication. The passkey 'error' is fabricated to force password entry.

sourcePatterns.description: Phone number with country code dropdown and verification — PII harvesting for SIM swap
sourcePatterns.note: Phone number fields with country code dropdowns combined with 'verify/confirm' buttons harvest full international phone numbers. This data enables SIM swap attacks, vishing, and SMS-based attacks.

sourcePatterns.description: Full address form with autocomplete harvesting — PII collection for identity theft
sourcePatterns.note: Full address forms collecting street, city, state, and zip with a submit action harvest complete physical address data. Combined with other PII fields, this enables identity theft and account takeover.

sourcePatterns.description: Hidden form fields designed to capture browser autofill data — silent credential harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: Hidden input fields with autocomplete attributes trick browsers into auto-filling credit card, name, email, phone, and address data. The fields are invisible to the user but the browser fills them automatically, enabling silent PII harvesting.

sourcePatterns.description: Document designMode enabled for credential capture — entire page made editable
sourcePatterns.note: Setting document.designMode='on' makes the entire page editable like a text editor. Phishing kits use this to create input areas without standard form elements, bypassing form-based detection. Any text entered anywhere on the page can be captured.

sourcePatterns.description: Credential Management API used to store or request credentials — API abuse for credential theft
sourcePatterns.note: Abusing the Credential Management API (navigator.credentials.get/store) to request stored credentials or create federated credentials is a sophisticated phishing technique. The browser's native credential prompt is trusted by users.

sourcePatterns.description: Mobile phone number input with specific mobile validation patterns — smishing landing page
sourcePatterns.note: Phone number input fields with tel type or numeric inputmode combined with verification actions are characteristic of smishing landing pages that collect phone numbers for SIM swap attacks or further targeting.

sourcePatterns.description: SMS OTP verification code input UI with auto-read capability — MFA interception page
sourcePatterns.note: Using the Web OTP API (OTPCredential) or autocomplete='one-time-code' with navigator.credentials.get to auto-read SMS verification codes is used by phishing pages to silently intercept MFA codes sent to the victim's phone.

sourcePatterns.description: Chain of single-digit OTP input fields with auto-focus — SMS code interception UI
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript that auto-advances focus between single-digit input fields with OTP/code/verify context creates the 6-digit verification code entry UI. This auto-focus chain combined with verification text is a phishing MFA intercept indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form only visible at mobile breakpoints — desktop hides credential harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: CSS media queries that hide login forms on desktop breakpoints (min-width >= 768px) while showing them on mobile create mobile-only phishing pages. Desktop-based security scanners see no form at all.

sourcePatterns.description: Camera/microphone permission requested on phishing page — surveillance or document capture
sourcePatterns.note: Requesting camera/microphone access on a credential harvesting page is used for selfie-based verification scams (fake KYC/ID verification) or to record the victim entering credentials. Legitimate login pages don't request camera access.

sourcePatterns.description: NFC/contactless payment API interaction on phishing page — payment data interception
sourcePatterns.note: Interacting with NFC/NDEF APIs to read payment card data or write malicious payment tags is a mobile-specific credential harvesting technique targeting tap-to-pay functionality.

sourcePatterns.description: Fine GPS location harvested alongside credentials — precise victim location tracking
sourcePatterns.note: Requesting fine GPS location alongside credential capture gives attackers the victim's exact physical location. This data enables physical attacks (home break-in, stalking) or verifies victim identity for targeted fraud.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript bridge to native app WebView detected — credential interception in app context
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript bridges to native WebView contexts (webkit.messageHandlers for iOS, AndroidBridge for Android) can intercept credentials entered in app-embedded browsers. Phishing pages detect they're in a WebView and use the bridge to exfiltrate data.

sourcePatterns.description: Fetch POST to Telegram API with credential data in body — direct exfiltration call
sourcePatterns.note: A fetch() POST to any URL containing 'telegram' with credential-related fields in the body is a direct Telegram exfiltration call. This catches both plain and partially obfuscated API URLs.

sourcePatterns.description: XMLHttpRequest to Telegram API — legacy exfiltration method still widely used
sourcePatterns.note: XMLHttpRequest-based Telegram API calls are used by older phishing kits and infostealers. While fetch() is more common in modern kits, many still use XHR for compatibility with older browsers.

sourcePatterns.description: Multipart form data constructed for Telegram file upload exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Constructing a FormData object with file/document/photo fields for Telegram API upload is the file exfiltration preparation pattern. The FormData is sent to sendDocument or sendPhoto endpoints.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook embed with credential field names — structured exfiltration report
sourcePatterns.note: Discord webhook embeds with fields containing credential labels create formatted exfiltration reports. Each embed field displays a stolen credential (email, password, IP, etc.) in a structured Discord message.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook POST with file attachment — exfiltrating credential files via Discord
sourcePatterns.note: Sending files via Discord webhooks using multipart FormData with payload_json is used to exfiltrate credential archives, screenshots, and cookie databases. Discord allows 8MB file attachments per webhook message.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram Bot API sendLocation — victim GPS coordinates exfiltrated to attacker
sourcePatterns.note: The sendLocation API endpoint sends GPS coordinates to a Telegram chat. Phishing kits that harvest geolocation data use this to send the victim's exact location to the attacker alongside credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook with multiple embeds containing different credential categories
sourcePatterns.note: Multiple Discord embeds in a single webhook call, each containing different credential categories (login info, payment data, personal info), create a comprehensive victim profile report sent as a single formatted message.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook simple content message with credential data — plaintext exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Simple Discord webhook messages with plaintext content containing 'email:', 'password:', etc. are basic credential exfiltration. Not all phishkits use embeds — many just send a plain text message with credential key:value pairs.

sourcePatterns.description: Form with absolute URL action and any text+password input combo — keyword-free credential harvest detection
sourcePatterns.note: Detects credential forms by STRUCTURE not keywords: a POST form with absolute cross-origin action containing both a text/email input and a password input. Catches phish even when all field names/IDs are randomized.

sourcePatterns.description: Page with 2+ password input fields — multi-password harvesting or password change phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Two password fields on the same page is unusual for legitimate login (which has one). It indicates either a 'current + new password' phishing page or a double-entry verification trick. Doesn't rely on field names.

sourcePatterns.description: Input field .value read followed by HTTP POST within 1000 chars — keyword-free exfiltration behavior
sourcePatterns.note: Reading any input .value then making an external HTTP request within 1000 chars is the behavioral skeleton of credential exfiltration. Works regardless of field names — catches .value → fetch() even with randomized variable names.

sourcePatterns.description: getElementById followed by external POST — structural credential read-then-send pattern
sourcePatterns.note: The getElementById → .value → POST chain is the fundamental credential harvesting behavior regardless of what the element IDs are named. Catches keyword-avoidance evasion.

sourcePatterns.description: querySelector targeting input elements followed by value extraction — generic field harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: Using querySelector('input[...]') to select form inputs by type/attribute rather than name, then extracting .value and sending externally. This behavioral pattern survives complete field name randomization.

sourcePatterns.description: Credentials sent to a Cloudflare Worker endpoint — proxy-based exfiltration hiding final destination
sourcePatterns.note: Sending credential data to a workers.dev or pages.dev endpoint uses Cloudflare as a proxy. The Worker forwards credentials to the actual C2 (Telegram, Discord, email, custom server). This defeats direct endpoint matching.

sourcePatterns.description: Input type changed at runtime from text to password or vice versa — evasion of type=password detection
sourcePatterns.note: Changing an input field's type from 'text' to 'password' at runtime defeats static HTML scanning for type=password. The field appears as a text input in the source but becomes a password field when JavaScript executes.

sourcePatterns.description: Input field name/id set dynamically at runtime — avoids static field name matching
sourcePatterns.note: Setting input field names/IDs dynamically using decoded strings avoids static source analysis of field names. The decoded name might be 'password' but it never appears as a literal string in the source.

sourcePatterns.description: Form with no action attribute but JS submit handler — action set dynamically to avoid static analysis
sourcePatterns.note: A form with password input but NO action attribute, followed immediately by a script tag, means the form submission is entirely controlled by JavaScript. The exfil destination is set dynamically and never appears in the HTML.

sourcePatterns.description: Minimal HTML page with only a form and few elements — stripped-down phishing with no JS
sourcePatterns.note: A minimal HTML page (DOCTYPE → body → form → password → /body with little else) is a stripped-down phishing page with no JavaScript at all. All logic is server-side, making client-side detection extremely difficult. The minimalism itself is the signal.

sourcePatterns.description: All form inputs collected into object/array on submit — bulk data harvesting regardless of field names
sourcePatterns.note: Iterating over ALL form inputs and collecting their values into a data structure is a bulk harvesting technique that captures everything regardless of field names. The kit doesn't need to know field names — it just grabs all input values.

sourcePatterns.description: FormData constructed from entire form element — captures all fields without knowing names
sourcePatterns.note: new FormData(formElement) serializes ALL fields in a form automatically — the kit doesn't need to reference individual field names. This keyword-independent harvesting captures everything including hidden fields and CSRF tokens.

sourcePatterns.description: MutationObserver watching for input value changes — passive credential monitoring without event handlers
sourcePatterns.note: Using MutationObserver to passively watch for input value changes avoids attaching suspicious event listeners to form fields. The observer silently captures everything typed without any addEventListener or onkeypress.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript Proxy handler trapping property access — intercepts credential reads transparently
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript Proxy objects with get/set traps can transparently intercept any property access on form elements. When the legitimate page code reads input.value, the Proxy intercepts it and exfiltrates the value before returning it.

sourcePatterns.description: Event listener attached to all input elements via querySelectorAll — bulk keystroke monitoring
sourcePatterns.note: Attaching event listeners to ALL input elements via querySelectorAll('input').forEach(addEventListener) is bulk credential monitoring. It captures everything typed in any field without needing to know specific field selectors.

sourcePatterns.description: Data exfiltrated via dynamically set CSS background-image URL — non-network-request exfil
sourcePatterns.note: Setting a CSS background-image URL that includes credential data exfiltrates via an image request that doesn't look like a fetch/XHR call. The browser loads the 'image' which is actually a tracking pixel URL containing the stolen data.

sourcePatterns.description: Image src dynamically set with credential data in URL — pixel-based exfiltration bypass
sourcePatterns.note: Creating an Image element and setting its src to a URL that includes form input .value data exfiltrates via an image request. This bypass technique doesn't use fetch/XHR/sendBeacon — the browser simply tries to 'load' the image containing the stolen data in the URL.

sourcePatterns.description: cf-turnstile-response token submitted alongside credential fields — Turnstile-gated phishing exfil
sourcePatterns.note: The cf-turnstile-response token being collected and submitted alongside credential field values confirms that Turnstile is being used to gate a credential harvesting form. The token proves the victim is human; the credentials are the payload.

sourcePatterns.description: Turnstile widget protecting a form with cross-origin action URL — credential exfil behind CAPTCHA gate
sourcePatterns.note: A Turnstile widget on the same page as a form with a cross-origin action URL containing a password field is almost certainly phishing. Legitimate sites that use Turnstile submit to their own domain, not to an external endpoint.

sourcePatterns.description: Turnstile token extracted and sent to external server alongside credentials — AiTM token relay
sourcePatterns.note: Extracting the Turnstile token and sending it to an external server alongside credentials is the AiTM relay pattern. The attacker uses the victim's solved Turnstile token to bypass CAPTCHA on the real site while stealing credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Hidden input named cf-turnstile-response alongside credential inputs — Turnstile token in exfil payload
sourcePatterns.note: A hidden input named cf-turnstile-response on the same form as a password field confirms Turnstile is gating a credential harvesting form. The token is submitted alongside the stolen credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft ESTSAUTH or ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT cookie captured in JavaScript — session token theft
sourcePatterns.note: ESTSAUTH and ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT are Microsoft's Entra ID session cookies. Finding these cookie names in JavaScript on a non-Microsoft domain means a phishing page is capturing Microsoft session tokens for replay. This is the core AiTM session hijacking indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: document.cookie read and sent to external endpoint — bulk session cookie exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Reading document.cookie and sending it externally via any channel (fetch, XHR, sendBeacon, postMessage, Image, WebSocket) is the fundamental AiTM session cookie exfiltration pattern. The stolen cookies allow session replay without re-authentication.
