Page on duckdns.org free DDNS contains an HTML form — extremely high phishing correlation
Venmo-branded PHP logging endpoint in URL structure
sourcePatterns.note: Detects Venmo-specific credential harvesting PHP endpoints in HTML attributes

sourcePatterns.description: Venmo-branded URL with PHP credential harvesting endpoint and randomized session path
sourcePatterns.note: Specific to Venmo phishkits using PHP backends with characteristic naming conventions - legitimate Venmo never uses PHP endpoints

sourcePatterns.description: Base64-encoded email address detected in URL parameter within page links
sourcePatterns.note: Base64-encoded values in URL parameters named email/user/login/id/token indicate personalized phishing where the victim's email is encoded in the URL for tracking or form pre-fill.

sourcePatterns.description: Form action URL pointing to a suspicious TLD domain — exfiltration to cheap/free domains
sourcePatterns.note: Form actions pointing to domains on suspicious TLDs (.top, .xyz, .buzz, .live, .icu, .tk, etc.) indicate credential exfiltration to cheap/free domain infrastructure. These TLDs are disproportionately used for phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript redirect to suspicious TLD — phishing redirect chain endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript redirects to suspicious TLDs indicate the page is part of a phishing redirect chain that bounces through multiple domains.

sourcePatterns.description: Multiple sequential redirects in JavaScript — phishing redirect chain
sourcePatterns.note: Two or more redirect assignments in the same script indicate a redirect chain. Phishing kits bounce through multiple intermediary pages before landing on the final credential harvesting page.

sourcePatterns.description: URL shortener used in JavaScript redirect — obscured phishing destination
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting through URL shorteners in JavaScript hides the final phishing destination from both users and URL-based detection. No legitimate login page redirects through shortener services.

sourcePatterns.description: DuckDNS dynamic DNS domain in page links — free DNS commonly abused for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: DuckDNS is a free dynamic DNS service heavily abused for phishing infrastructure. The .duckdns.org subdomain indicates attacker-controlled dynamic DNS infrastructure.

sourcePatterns.description: Google open redirect URLs used in page source — abuses Google's trusted domain for redirect
sourcePatterns.note: Using Google's open redirect endpoints (google.com/url?q=, google.com/amp/s/) to bounce through Google's domain makes phishing links appear to come from Google. This abuses Google's trusted reputation.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft open redirect URLs used in page source — abuses Microsoft's trusted domain
sourcePatterns.note: Abusing Microsoft's open redirectors to bounce phishing links through Microsoft's trusted domain. Common in BEC campaigns targeting Office 365 users.

sourcePatterns.description: Custom app deep link schemes in page source — potential app-based phishing redirect
sourcePatterns.note: Custom URI schemes for messaging apps (whatsapp://, telegram://, signal://) in page links can deep-link into apps with pre-filled malicious content, contact additions, or group invites used in social engineering chains.

sourcePatterns.description: Chinese .TOP domain in page source links — Smishing Triad infrastructure indicator
sourcePatterns.note: The Smishing Triad predominantly uses .TOP domains for their toll road, delivery, and payment phishing campaigns. Links to .top domains combined with logistics/toll/payment keywords are characteristic of this Chinese smishing group.

sourcePatterns.description: Link-in-bio service used as phishing redirect — abuses social media link tools
sourcePatterns.note: Link-in-bio services (Linktree, Beacons, Stan, Bio.link) are abused as phishing redirectors because their domains are trusted and rarely blocked. The phishing link hides behind a legitimate social media link tool.

sourcePatterns.description: Footer navigation links pointing to # or javascript:void — phishing page with non-functional navigation
sourcePatterns.note: Two or more footer/navigation links pointing to # or javascript:void(0) indicates a phishing page with decorative non-functional links. Phishers copy the visual layout of legitimate sites but don't bother making footer links work.

sourcePatterns.description: Base href tag pointing to a different domain — makes relative URLs resolve to attacker's server
sourcePatterns.note: A <base href> tag pointing to an external domain makes ALL relative URLs on the page resolve to that domain instead of the current one. Phishers use this to load assets from a different server while hosting the phishing page elsewhere.

sourcePatterns.description: Page with empty, blank, or extremely generic title — phishing pages often lack proper titles
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing pages often have generic, blank, or single-word titles like 'Document', 'Login', 'Verify', 'Untitled'. Legitimate login pages always have branded titles (e.g., 'Sign in to Microsoft'). A generic title on a credential page is suspicious.

sourcePatterns.description: Page resources loaded from an IP address rather than a domain name — suspicious hosting indicator
sourcePatterns.note: Loading page resources (scripts, images, form actions) from raw IP addresses instead of domain names is a hosting indicator. Legitimate sites use domain names; phishing infrastructure often uses IP addresses to avoid domain takedowns.

sourcePatterns.description: Login page with no navigation menu or site links — isolated credential form with no site context
sourcePatterns.note: A page with only a login form and no navigation menu, header links, or site structure is an isolated credential form. Legitimate login pages exist within a site with navigation to home, help, terms, etc. Phishing pages are stripped to just the form.

sourcePatterns.description: Form action URL using a cheap/suspicious TLD — .xyz, .top, .tk, .ml, .ga, .cf, .gq hosting
sourcePatterns.note: Form actions posting to cheap or commonly-abused TLDs (.xyz, .top, .tk, .ml, .ga, .cf, .gq, .icu, etc.) are strong phishing indicators. These TLDs are disproportionately used for phishing due to low cost or free registration.

sourcePatterns.description: Multiple anchor tags with empty href — decorative non-functional links on phishing page
sourcePatterns.note: Three or more anchor tags with empty href attributes on the same page are decorative links that go nowhere. Phishing pages include visual link text (Terms, Privacy, Help) but with empty hrefs because the phisher didn't set up those pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Meta refresh tag with delayed redirect — auto-redirect to phishing page or away from evidence
sourcePatterns.note: A meta refresh tag with a URL redirect can auto-redirect to a phishing page from a compromised/trusted site, or redirect away from the phishing page after credential capture to destroy evidence.

sourcePatterns.description: Page where all hyperlinks point to external domains — no internal navigation structure
sourcePatterns.note: A page where all links are absolute URLs to external domains (no relative internal links) indicates a page without internal site structure. Phishing pages link out to the real brand site for Terms/Privacy but have no internal pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirect through trusted intermediate service before phishing landing — Tycoon's redirect laundering
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon chains redirects through trusted services (Firebase, Azure Blob, Wix, TikTok, Google Docs/Sites) before landing on the phishing page. These trusted intermediate domains lend legitimacy to the redirect chain and bypass URL reputation checks.

sourcePatterns.description: Domain using .xin TLD in page resources — Smishing Triad toll phishing infrastructure indicator
sourcePatterns.note: The .xin TLD (Chinese for 'new/faith', managed by Elegant Leader Limited in Hong Kong) is specifically used by Smishing Triad for toll phishing domains. Resecurity identified thousands of toll phishing domains registered under .xin.
